titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Lillian Spender, 24 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) is writing to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). In this letter, Spender gives a brief account of his arrival at the Corps Headquarters, of his longing for more letters, and of his guilt over abandoning his brother Rex.Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
24 July My Pr I have arrived - early yesterday morning - at the Corps HQ, but am
only now getting into the work at all properly. Some people have the
gift of handing over, others with the best will in the world have not.
This corps has been the most successful hitherto, & therefore its work
should be on sound lines, & it is interesting seeing a new formation at
work tho' it lacks the excitement of a Divisional office. I believe now that my message to Sr edward was not correct, tho
true of the actual situation. There appears to be the greatest difficulty
in getting the stuff out tho' we are assured there is plenty at home. One's facilities for writing letters are worse than ever before as 2
of us sit at the same table - not always the same 2 tho' now I have more
or less settled my own place. This & having no one letters to inspire me will
make this a very dull letter. My work seems likely to be mainly in the office but I hope to get some exercise daily. I did not say goodbye to GN as he was out & declined to
say goodbye on the previous day. I am not sure that this was not
deliberate on his part. In any case I really cannot say I am sorry
to leave him. He is I think the most naturally selfish man I have ever
been brought into close touch with, & really this alleviates my regret
to some extent, tho' I still feel as if I had deserted Rex in his hour of need
It is, I am told absolutely certain that the only excuse for refusing any
appointment is lack of qualification & tho' it is quite likely I may show
myself unfitted after trial I am not going to admit it before. No time for more as I am off to visit the 'German'
lines in a motor ! Address Lt Col GS. attached HQ 15 Corps. Your b w
WB Spender PS: Some folks put RA & omit GS on my letters which
may lead to my not getting them now.
